title: Langevin Dynamics of Yang-Mills in 2D and 3D
speaker: Hao Shen
abstract: We will discuss stochastic quantization i.e. Langevin dynamic of the Yang-Mills model on two and three dimensional torus. This is described by a Lie algebra valued stochastic PDE driven by space-time white noise. We construct (local) solution to this singular SPDE via a limit of smooth regularizations; gauge invariance is broken in the regularizations but is restored in the limit. This then defines a Markov process on the space of gauge orbits.

proof_stages.label: Gauge-orbit formulation of stochastic Yang-Mills
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames 2D and 3D stochastic Yang-Mills as an infinite-dimensional, gauge-symmetric analogue of Brownian motion modulo rotations, defining curvature, gauge transformations, Wilson loops, and the Langevin SPDE whose solutions should descend to gauge orbits.

proof_stages.label: Two-dimensional singular state and orbit spaces
proof_stages.summary: A tailored space Ω is constructed between continuous functions and C^{0-} distributions, using line-segment and triangle-boundary norms so that Gaussian-free-field-type connections are supported and Wilson loops and gauge transformations remain meaningful.

proof_stages.label: Three-dimensional flow-regularized Wilson loops and gauge equivalence
proof_stages.summary: Because 3D Gaussian-free-field singularities cannot be integrated along curves, Wilson loops are regularized by deterministic Yang-Mills flow, which requires a nonlinear state space with enough time-integrability to solve the singular initial-value flow and define gauge equivalence through its smoothed images.

proof_stages.label: Local solution theory and renormalization in two dimensions
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing scalar toy SPDEs, the 2D Yang-Mills Langevin equation is solved locally by decomposing the field into its Gaussian linear part plus a better remainder described by regularity structures, with the finitely many counterterms collapsing to a mass renormalization.

proof_stages.label: Restoration of gauge invariance by fixing the mass counterterm
proof_stages.summary: Although the approximation and mass renormalization break gauge symmetry, a pathwise gauge transform of one solution leads to a transformed equation in which the noise correction and mass-counterterm defect match, giving a unique finite shift for which the limiting orbit process is gauge invariant.

proof_stages.label: Three-dimensional renormalization and symmetry reduction
proof_stages.summary: The 3D construction extends the SPDE renormalization theory to Lie-algebra-valued equations, uses discrete symmetries to reduce hundreds of algebraic counterterms to a single mass term, and couples the field, gauge transform, and transformed field to prove the corresponding gauge-invariant limit.

proof_stages.label: Lattice Langevin consequences for large-scale questions
proof_stages.summary: On the lattice, Langevin SDEs give a short Itô-formula proof of Wilson-loop recursions and, at strong coupling for SO and SU groups, functional inequalities implying uniqueness of the invariant measure, large-N factorization, and an exponential mass gap.

proof_stages.label: Scope and obstructions beyond the local continuum result
proof_stages.summary: The Q&A identifies barriers to weak-coupling, long-time, and whole-space continuum results: Bakry-Émery curvature bounds fail at weak coupling, and the stochastic decompositions needed for renormalization obscure the gradient-flow a priori estimates that work in deterministic Yang-Mills.

examples.name: Rotationally invariant Brownian motion modulo spheres
examples.note: The rotation quotient provides the finite-dimensional model for a gauge-orbit Markov process and later for proving invariance by pathwise rotations.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces Brownian motion from points on the same sphere through projection to orbit space and checks the induced process and norm observable are rotation-invariant

examples.name: Abelian gauge-symmetric linear SPDE
examples.note: The Abelian vector-field SPDE makes the loop integral gauge invariant and shows why adding a gradient-divergence DeTurck drift can restore parabolicity without changing orbit dynamics.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes the linear gauge-symmetric SPDE, checks invariance under adding a gradient, checks loop integrals are invariant, and adds the DeTurck-type drift to make it parabolic

examples.name: Gauge-breaking mollifier and heat-flow regularizations
examples.note: Convolution or the heat semigroup would smooth a 3D connection but destroy gauge invariance, motivating the deterministic Yang-Mills flow as the right regularizer.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker compares convolution and heat-flow regularizations by checking that they smooth but break gauge invariance, then analyzes the Yang-Mills-flow regularization and its singular-initial-data PDE obstruction
